local_customs: No tipping: Excellent service is standard, tipping is considered awkward or offensive.
Escalator etiquette: Stand on the left, let others walk on the right (in Tokyo).
Quiet on transit: Avoid talking loudly or answering phone calls on trains.
Trash disposal: Keep your trash with you; public bins are extremely rare.
Cash is king: Although credit cards are increasingly accepted, keep JPY cash on hand.
Greeting standard: Always start interactions with a polite 'Bonjour' or 'Bonsoir'.
Table manners: Hands should remain visible on the table, not in your lap.
Tipping: A 15% service charge is included in bills, but leaving 1-2 Euros for good service is appreciated.
Metro vigilance: Watch your belongings, pickpocketing is common in tourist spots.
Pace of dining: Mealtime is considered leisure; don't expect waiters to rush you.
Dress code: Dress modestly when visiting churches; cover shoulders and knees.
Coffee ordering: Drink espresso at the bar counter; sitting down at tables costs significantly more.
Tipping: Not mandatory, but leaving small change (5-10%) is considered polite.
Water fountains: You can drink from any of the public 'nasoni' water fountains around Rome for free.
Dinner timing: Locals eat late, typically starting around 8:30 PM or 9:00 PM.
Queueing: Respect queues; cutting in line is considered highly offensive.
Escalator etiquette: Stand on the right, walk on the left.
Tipping: 10-12.5% is often added to restaurant bills as service charge. Otherwise, tip 10%.
Umbrella ready: Keep a small umbrella handy; weather changes quickly.
Mind the Gap: Pay attention to the space between the train door and platform on the Tube.
Tipping: Standard tipping is 18-20% in restaurants, $1-2 per drink at bars, and 15-20% for taxis.
Pedestrian pace: Walk quickly and pull over to the side if you need to look at maps/photos.
Subway swipe: Make sure you have a MetroCard or use contactless tap (OMNY) before entering.
Jaywalking: Extremely common, but cross carefully at crosswalks.
Tax: Sales tax (8.875%) is added at checkout, not shown on price tags.
Temple wear: Wear a sarong and sash when entering temples (usually rentable at entrance).
Right hand rules: Use your right hand for giving, receiving, or eating.
Offerings: Do not step on 'Canang Sari' (small palm-leaf baskets of flower offerings) on the street.
Bartering: Polite bargaining is common in local markets, but not in supermarkets or malls.
Drinking water: Tap water is unsafe. Only drink bottled or filtered water ('Bali Belly' protection).
Sun protection: The Australian sun is intense. Use sunscreen (SPF 50+), hats, and sunglasses.
Tipping: Not expected, but round up the bill or tip 10% for exceptional service.
Beach safety: Swim only between the red and yellow flags. Obey lifeguards.
Greeting style: Casual 'G'day' or 'How's it going' is normal.
Strict borders: Do not bring food, seeds, or organic materials into Australia; biosecurity is strict.
